question: 7. Statement: Should young entrepreneurs be encouraged? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!flalign= lalign] item Yes. They will help in industrial development of the country. item Yes. They will reduce the burden on employment market. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: Clearly, encouraging the young entrepreneurs will open up the field for the establishment of new industries. Thus, it shall help in industrial development and not only employ the entrepreneurs but create more job opportunities for others as well. So, both the arguments hold strong.

question: Statement: Should all the annual examinations up to Std. V be abolished? Arguments:  eginenumerate item Yes. The young students should not be burdened with such examinations which hampers their natural growth. item No. The students will not study seriously as they will get automatic promotion to the n class and this will affect them in future. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: Clearly, neither the students can be burdened with studies at such a tender age, nor can they be free to take studies casually, as this shall weaken their basic foundation. So, both the arguments follow.

question: 9. Statement: Should Indian scientists working abroad be called back to India? Arguments:  eginenumerate item Yes. They must serve the motherland first and forget about discoveries, honours, facilities and all. item No. We have enough talent; let them stay where they want. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: Neither I nor II is strong
explanation: Clearly, every person must be free to work wherever he wants and no compulsion should be made to confine one to one's own country. So, argument I is vague. However, talented scientists can be of great benefit to the nation and some alternatives as special incentives or better prospects may be made available to them to retain them within their motherland. So, argument II also does not hold.

question: Statement: Should we scrap the system of formal education beyond graduation? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Yes. It will mean taking employment at an early date. item No. It will mean lack of depth of knowledge. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Clearly, argument I is vague because at present too, many fields are open to all after graduation. However, eliminating the post-graduate courses would abolish higher and specialized studies which lead to understanding things better and deeply. So, argument II is valid.

question: 11. Statement: Should there be an upper age limit of 65 years for contesting Parliamentary/ Legislative Assembly elections? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Yes. Generally, people above the age of 65 lose their dynamism and will power. item No. The life span is so increased that people remain physically and mentally active even up to the age of 80. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: Neither I nor II is strong
explanation: The age of a person is no criterion for judging his mental capabilities and administrative qualities. So, none of the arguments holds strong.

question: Statement: Should new big industries be started in Mumbai? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Yes. It will create job opportunities. item No. It will further add to the pollution of the city. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: Either I or II is strong
explanation: Opening up of new industries is advantageous in opening more employment avenues, and disadvantageous in that it adds to the pollution. So, either of the arguments holds strong.

question: 13. Statement: Should high chimneys be installed in industries? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Yes. It reduces pollution at ground level. item No. It increases pollution in upper atmosphere. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Pollution at ground level is the most hazardous in the way of being injurious to human and animal life. So, argument I alone holds.

question: 14. Statement: Does India need so many plans for development? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Yes. Nothing can be achieved without proper planning. item No. Too much time, money and energy is wasted on planning. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Before indulging in new development programme it is much necessary to plan the exact target, policies and their implementation and the allocation of funds which shows the r direction to work. So, argument I holds strong. Also, planning ensures full utilization of available resources and funds and stepwise approach towards the target. So, spending a part of money on it is no wastage. Thus, argument II is not valid.

question: 15. Statement: Should articles of only deserving authors be allowed to be published? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Yes. It will save a lot of paper which is in short supply. item No. It is not possible to draw a line between the deserving and the undeserving. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Clearly, I does not provide a strong reason in support of the statement. Also, it is not possible to analyze the really deserving and not deserving. So/argument II holds strong.

question: 16. Statement: Should colleges be given the status of a university in India? Arguments:  eginflalign* & i. Yes. Colleges are in a better position to assess the student's performance and therefore the degrees will be more valid.& & ii. No. It is Utopian to think that there will not be nepotism and corruption in awarding degrees by colleges.& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: Neither I nor II is strong
explanation: Clearly, at the college level, all the students are assessed according to their performance in the University Exams and not on the basis of any criteria of a more intimate dealings with the students. So, argument I is vague. Also, at this level the awarding of degrees is impartial and simply based on his performance. So, argument II also does not hold.

question: 17. Statement: Should the prestigious people who have committed crime unknowingly, be met with special treatment? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Yes. The prestigious people do not commit crime intentionally. item No. It is our policy that everybody is equal before the law. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: The Constitution of India has laid down the doctrine of 'equality before the law'. So, argument II holds strong. Also, we cannot judge the intentions of a person behind committing a crime, So, argument I is vague.

question: 18. Statement: Can pollution be controlled? Arguments:  eginenumerate item Yes. If everyone realizes the hazards it may create and cooperates to get rid of it, pollution may be controlled. item No. The crowded highways, factories and industries and an ever-growing population eager to acquire more and more land for constructing houses are beyond control. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: Either I or II is strong
explanation: The control of pollution, on one hand, seems to be impossible because of the ever-growing needs and the disconcern of the people but, on the other hand, the control is possible by a joint effort. So, either of the arguments will hold strong.

question: 19.~ Statement: Should the railways in India be privatized in a phased manner like other public sector enterprises? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!I] item Yes. This is the only way to bring in competitiveness and provide better services to the public. item No. This will pose a threat to the national security of our country as multinationals will enter into the fray. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: Neither I nor II is strong
explanation: Privatization would no doubt lead to better services. But saying that this is the 'only way' is wrong. So, argument I does not hold. Argument II also seems to be vague.

question: 20. Statement: Should internal assessment in colleges be abolished? Arguments:  eginenumerate item Yes. This will help in reducing the possibility of favouritism. item No, teaching faculty will lose control over students. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Abolishing the internal assessment would surely reduce favouritism on personal grounds because the teachers would not be involved in examination system so that they cannot end personal benefits to anyone. So, argument I holds strong. But it will not affect the control of teaching faculty on students because still the teachers would be teaching them. So, argument II is vague.

question: 21. Statement: Should all the unauthorized structures in the city be demolished? Arguments:  eginenumerate item No. Where will the people residing in such houses live? item Yes. This will give a clear message to general public and they will refrain from constructing unauthorized buildings. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: The demolition of unauthorized buildings would teach a lesson to the unscrupulous builders and also serve as a warning for the citizens not to indulge in such activities in the future. This is essential, as unauthorized constructions impose undue burden on the city's infrastructure. So, only argument II holds strong.

question: 22. Statement: Should there be a maximum limit for the number of ministers in the Central Government? Arguments:  eginenumerate item No. The political party in power should have the freedom to decide the number of ministers to be appointed. item Yes. The number of ministers should be restricted to a certain percentage of the total number of seats in the parliament to avoid unnecessary expenditure. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Clearly, there should be some norms regarding the number of ministers in the Government, as more number of ministers would unnecessarily add to the Government expenditure. So, argument II holds strong; Also, giving liberty to the party in power could promote ension of unreasonable favour to some people at the cost of government funds. So, argument I does not hold.

question: 23. Statement: Should foreign films be banned in India? Arguments:  eginenumerate item Yes. They depict an alien culture which adversely affects our values. item No. Foreign films are of a high artistic standard. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: Neither I nor II is strong
explanation: Clearly, foreign films depict the alien culture but this only helps in learning more. So, argument I does not hold. Also, the reason stated in argument II is not strong enough in contradicting the ban. So, it also does not hold.

question: 24. Statement: Is buying things on instalments profitable to the customer? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!I] item Yes. He has to pay less. item No, paying instalments upsets the family budget. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: Neither I nor II is strong
explanation: In buying things on instalments, a customer has to pay more as the interest is also included. So, argument I does not hold. Moreover, one who buys an item on instalments maintains his future budget accordingly as he is well acquainted with when and how much he has to pay, beforehand. So, argument II is also not valid.

question: 25. Statement: Should Doordarshan be given autonomous status? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Yes. It will help Doordarshan to have fair and impartial coverage of all important events. item No. The coverage of events will be decided by a few who may not have healthy outlook. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Clearly, the autonomous status of the Doordarshan will be a step towards giving it independence for an impartial coverage. Autonomous status does not mean that the coverage will be decided by a few. So, only argument I holds.

question: 26. Statement: Should adult education programme be given priority over compulsory education programme? Arguments:  eginenumerate item No. It will also help in success of compulsory education programme. item Yes. It will help to eliminate the adult illite/y. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Clearly, argument I gives a reason in support of the statement and so it does not hold strong against it. The adult education programme needs to be given priority because it shall eliminate adult illite/y and thus help in further spread of education. So, only argument II is strong enough.

question: 27. Statement: Should new universities be established in India? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item No. We have still not achieved the target for lite/y. item No. We will have to face the problem of unemployed but highly qualified people. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: Clearly, instead of improving upon higher education, increasing the lite/y rate should be heeded first. So, argument I holds. Also, more number of universities will produce more degree holders with the number of jobs remaining the same, thus increasing unemployment. So, argument II also holds strong.

question: 28. Statement: Should non-vegetarian food be totally banned in our country? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!<arabic*>] item Yes. It is expensive and therefore it is beyond the means of most people in our country. item No. Nothing should be banned in a democratic country like ours. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Clearly, restriction on the diet of people will be denying them their basic human r. So, only argument II holds.

question: 29.~ Statement: Should a total ban be put on trapping wild animals? Arguments:  eginenumerate item Yes. Trappers are making a lot of money; item No. Bans on hunting and trapping are not effective. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: Neither I nor II is strong
explanation: Clearly, ban is necessary to protect our natural environment. So, none of the arguments is strong enough.

question: 30. Statement: Should Government close down loss-making public sector enterprises? Arguments:  eginflalign* & i. No. All employees will lose their jobs, security and earning, what would they do?& & ii. Yes. In a competitive world the rule is 'survival of the fittest'.& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Closing down public-sector enterprises will definitely throw the engaged persons out of employment. So, argument I holds. Also, closing down is no solution for a loss-making enterprise. Rather, its causes of failure should be studied, analyzed and the essential reforms implemented. Even if this does not work out, the enterprise may be privatized. So, argument II is vague.

question: 31.~ Statement: Should government jobs in rural areas have more incentives? Arguments:  eginenumerate item Yes. Incentives are essential for att/ting government servants there. item No. Rural areas are already cheaper, healthier and less complex than big cities. So ? Why offer ra incentives! endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Clearly, government jobs in rural areas are underlined with several difficulties. In lieu of these, ra incentives are needed. So, only argument I holds strong.

question: 32.~ Statement: Should there be a cap on maximum number of contestants for parliamentary elections in any constituency? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Yes. This will make the parliamentary elections more meaningful as the voters can make a considered judgement for casting their vote.~ item No. In a democ/y any person fulfilling the eligibility criteria can contest parliamentary elections and there should be no restrictions.~ endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: Clearly, if there were less candidates, the voters would find it easy to make a choice. So, argument I holds. Also, every person satisfying the conditions laid down by the Constitution must be given an opportunity and should not be denied the same just to cut down the number of candidates. So, argument II also holds strong.

question: 33.~ Statement: Should so much money be spent on advertisements? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Yes. It is an essential concomitant in a capitalist economy. item No. It leads to wastage of resources. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Clearly, the advertisements are/the means to introduce people with the product and its advantages. So, argument I holds strong. But argument II is vague because advertisements are an investment for better gain and not a, wastage.

question: 34.~ Statement: Should all the legislators be forced to resign from their profession? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Yes. They will be able to devote more time for the country. item No, nobody will contest election. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: The legislators should surely not be engaged in any other profession because only then will they be able to work with devotion. So, argument I holds. Also, if such a law is enforced, only those people will contest elections who are really prepared to work for the country. So, argument II is vague.

question: 35. Statement: Should 'computer knowledge' be made a compulsory subject for all the students at secondary school level? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item No, our need is 'bread' for everyone, we cannot follow western models. item Yes. We cannot compete in the international market without equipping our children with computers. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Nowadays, computers have entered all walks of life and children need to be prepared for the same. So, argument II is strong. Argument I holds no relevance.

question: 36.~ Statement: Should there be uniforms for students in the colleges in India as in the schools? Arguments:  eginflalign* & 1. Yes, this will improve the ambience of the colleges as all the students will be decently dressed.~ & 2. No. The college students should not be regimented and they should be to choose their clothes for coming to the college.~ endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Clearly, after being in strict discipline and following a formal dress code of the school for so many years, the students must be granted some liberty in college life, as they have to take on the responsibilities of life, n. Besides, schools adopt uniforms to take care of the security of the child - an aspect which doesn't matter much in the colleges. So, argument II holds strong. Also, the environment of the college depends on the students' dedication and etiquettes and not on their uniforms. So, argument I is vague.

question: 37. Statement: Should India engage into a dialogue with neighbouring countries to stop cross border tension? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Yes. This is the only way to reduce the cross border terrorism and stop loss of innocent lives. item No. Neighbouring countries cannot be relied upon in such matters, they may still engage in subversive activities. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Clearly, peaceful settlement through mutual agreement is the best option, whatever be the issue. So, argument I holds strong. Moreover, the problem indicated in II can be curbed by constant check and vigilance. So, II seems to be vague.

question: 38. Statement: Should there be a world government? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1.] item Yes. It will help in eliminating tensions among the nations. item No. Then, only the developed countries will dominate in the government. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Clearly, a world government cannot eliminate tensions among nations because it will also have the ruling group and the opposition group. Further, the more powerful and diplomatic shall rule the world to their interests. So, only argument II holds.

question: 39. Statement: Should the p/tice of transfers of clerical cadre employees from government offices of one city to those of another be stopped? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item No. Transfer of employees is a routine administrative matter and we must continue it. item Yes. It involves lot of governmental expenditure and inconvenience too many compared to the benefits it yields. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: Neither I nor II is strong
explanation: It is not necessary that any p/tice which has been in vogue for a long time is r and it must be continued. So, argument I is not strong. Also, a p/tice must be continued or discontinued in view of its merits/demerits and not on grounds of the expenditure or procedures it entails. The policy of transfer is generally p/tised to do away with corruption, which is absolutely essential. So, argument II also does not hold.

question: 40. Statement: Is paying ransom or agreeing to the conditions of kidnappers of political figures, a proper course of action? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1.] item Yes. The victims must be saved at all cost. item No. It encourages the kidnappers to continue their sinister activities. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: Both the arguments are strong enough. The conditions have to be agreed to, in order to save the life of the victims, though actually they ought not to be agreed to, as they encourage the sinister activities of the kidnappers.

question: 41.~ Statement: Should religion be banned? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Yes. It develops fanaticism in people. item No, Religion binds people together. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: Either I or II is strong
explanation: Religion binds people together through the name of God and human values. But at the same time it may create differences and ill-will among people. So, either of the arguments holds strong.

question: 42. bfStatement: Should India become a permanent member of UN's Security Council? bfArguments:  eginenumerate*[!label*=rabic*.] item Yes. India has emerged as a country which loves peace and amity. item No. Let us first solve problems of our own people like poverty, malnutrition. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: A peace-loving nation like India can well join an international forum which seeks to bring different nations on friendly terms with each other. So, argument I holds strong. Argument II highls a different aspect. The internal problems of a nation should not debar it from strengthening international ties. So, argument II is vague.

question: 43. Statement: Should fashionable dresses be banned? Arguments:  eginenumerate item Yes. Fashions keep changing and hence consumption of cloth increases. item No. Fashionable clothes are a person's self expression and therefore his/her fundamental r. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Clearly, imposing ban on fashionable dresses will be a restriction on the personal choice and hence the r to freedom of an individual. So, only argument II is strong.

question: 44. Statement: Should an organization like UNO be dissolved? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Yes. With cold war coming to an end, such organizations have no role to play. item No, In the absence of such organizations there may be a world war. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: An organization like UNO is meant to maintain peace all over and will always serve to prevent conflicts between countries. So, its role never ends. So, argument I does not hold. Also, lack of such an organization may in future lead to increased mutual conflicts and international wars, on account of lack of a common platform for mutual discussions. So, argument II holds.

question: 45. Statement: Should there be no place of interview in selection? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Yes, it is very subjective in assessment. item No. It is the only instrument to judge candidates' motives and personality. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Clearly, besides interview, there can be other modes of written examination to judge candidates' motives. So argument II is not strong enough. However, the interview is a subjective assessment without doubt. So, argument I holds.

question: 46. Statement: Should family planning be made compulsory in India? Arguments:  eginflalign* & 1. Yes. Looking to the miserable conditions in India, there is no other go.& & 2. No. In India there are people of various religions and family planning is against the tenets of some of the religions.& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: Family planning is an essential step to curb population growth. So, argument I holds strong. Also, family planning being against the tenets of some of the Indian religions, it is not necessary to make it compulsory. Instead, it can be enforced by creating public awareness of the benefits of family planning. So, argument II also holds.

question: 47. Statement: Should income tax be abolished in India? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Yes. It is an unnecessary burden on the wage earners. item No. It is a good source of revenue. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Income -tax is levied so that every citizen can contribute a share of his earning towards the infrastructural development of the nation. So, argument I seems to be vague. However, income-tax is no doubt a good source of revenue for the government. Hence, argument II holds strong.

question: 48. Statement: Should there be a ceiling on the salary of top executives of multinationals in our country? Arguments:  eginenumerate item Yes. Otherwise it would lead to unhealthy competition and our own industry would not be able to withstand that. item No. With the accent on liberalization of economy, any such move would be counter-productive. Once the economy picks up, this disparity will be reduced. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: In the absence of such a ceiling, the companies would be involved in a mutual competition of salaries, in a bid to att/t the most competent professionals. So, argument I holds. Also, the prospects of increase in salary would encourage the officials to perform better in the interest of the company they serve, which would otherwise not be so if a ceiling is imposed. So, argument II also holds strong.

question: 49. Statement: Should school education be made free in India? Arguments:  eginenumerate item Yes. This is the only way to improve the level of lite/y. item No. It would add to the already heavy burden on the exchequer. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Making education free for all is not the only means to ensure lite/y. An awareness needs to be aroused for this. So, argument I is vague. Also, such a step would require immense funds and lead to financial drain. So, argument II holds.

question: 50. Statement: Should import duty on all the electronic goods be dispensed with? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item No. This will considerably reduce the income of the government and will adversely affect the developmental activities. item No. The local manufacturers will not be able to compete with the foreign manufacturers who are technologically far superior. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Abolishing the import duty on electronic goods shall reduce the costs of imported goods and adversely affect the sale of the domestic products, thus giving a setback to the Indian electronics industry. So, argument II holds strong. Argument I does not provide a convincing reason.

question: 51. Statement: Should children be legally made responsible to take care of their parents during their old age? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Yes. Such matter can only be solved by legal means. item Yes. Only this will bring some relief to poor parents. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: Neither I nor II is strong
explanation: Taking care of the parents is a moral duty of the children and cannot be thrust upon them legally, nor such a compulsion can ensure good care of the old people. So, none of the arguments holds strong.

question: 52. Statement: Should there be reservation in Government jobs for candidates from single child family? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!1] item No. This is not advisable as the jobs should be offered to only deserving candidates without any reservation for a particular group. item Yes. This will help reduce the growing population in India as the parents will be encouraged to adopt single child norm. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: Neither I nor II is strong
explanation: The Government has already made provisions for reservation of jobs for the economically backward sections, which is a must. So, abolishing the p/tice of reservation altogether has no meaning. Thus, argument I is vague. Also, more reservations would lead to non-recruitment of many more deserving candidates. Besides, such a reservation, if implemented, will cater to the job requirements of only a small section of population and not a major part of it. So, argument II also does not hold strong.

question: 53.~ Statement: Should higher education be completely stopped for some time? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!I] item No. It will hamper the country's future progress. item Yes. It will reduce the educated unemployment. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
explanation: Clearly, higher education is not the cause of unemployment. In fact, it has created greater job opportunities. So, argument II is vague. Also, higher education promotes the country's development. So, argument I holds.

question: 54. Statement: Should we scrap the 'Public Distribution System' in India? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!I] item Yes, Protectionism is over, everyone must get the bread on his/her own. item Yes. The poor do not get any benefit because of corruption. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: Neither I nor II is strong
explanation: The Public Distribution System is indeed necessary to provide basic amenities to the economically backward sections of population. So, argument I is vague. Also, if the Objectives of a system are not fulfilled because of corruption, then getting rid of the system is no solution. Instead, efforts should be made to end corruption and end its benefits to the people for whom it is meant. So, argument II also does not hold.

question: 55.~ Statement: Should India have no military force at all? Arguments:  eginenumerate*[!r] item No. Other countries in the world do not believe in non-violence. item Yes. Many Indians believe in non-violence. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: Either I or II is strong
Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong
correct_answer: Neither I nor II is strong
explanation: Clearly, India needs to have military force to defend itself against the threat of other military powers in the world. So, none of the arguments holds strong.

question: Statements: No women teacher can play. Some women teachers are athletes. Conclusions:  eginflalign* & 1. Male athletes can play.& & 2. Some athletes can play.& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
explanation: Since one premise is negative, the conclusion must be negative. So, neither conclusion follows. Read: Important Rules and Formulas for Logical Deduction.

question: Statements: All bags are cakes. All lamps are cakes. Conclusions:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Some lamps are bags. item No lamp is bag. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

question: Statements: All mangoes are golden in colour. No golden-coloured things are cheap. Conclusions:  eginenumerate*[!1] item All mangoes are cheap. item Golden-coloured mangoes are not cheap. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
explanation: Clearly, the conclusion must be universal negative and should not contain the middle term. So, it follows that 'No mango is cheap'. Since all mangoes are golden in colour, we may substitute 'mangoes' with 'golden-coloured mangoes'. Thus, II follows.

question: 6. Statements: All roads are waters. Some waters are boats. Conclusions:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Some boats are roads. item All waters are boats. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
explanation: The first premise is A type and distributes the subject. So, the middle term 'waters' which forms its predicate, is not distributed. The second premise is I type and does not distribute either subject or predicate. So, the middle term 'waters' forming its subject is not distributed. Since the middle term is not distributed even once in the premises, no definite conclusion follows.

correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: As discussed above, the conclusion must be universal negative and should not contain the middle term. So, it follows that 'No flower is fruit'. I is the converse of this conclusion and thus it follows. II is the converse of the first premise and so it also holds.

question: 9. Statements: Every minister is a student. Every student is inexperienced. Conclusions:  eginenumerate[label= oman*.] item Every minister is inexperienced. item Some inexperienced are students. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: Every is equivalent to All. Thus, since both the premises are universal and affirmative, the conclusion must be universal affirmative and should not contain the middle term. So, I follows. II is the converse of the second premise and thus it also holds.

question: 12. Statements: Some dedicated souls are angels. All social workers are angels. Conclusions:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Some dedicated souls are social workers. item Some social workers are dedicated souls. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

question: 13.~ Statements: No gentleman is poor. All gentlemen are rich.~ Conclusions:  eginenumerate*[!1] item No poor man is rich.~ item No rich man is poor. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
explanation: The first premise is an E-type proposition, So, the middle term 'gentleman' forming the subject is distributed. The second premise is an A-type proposition. So, the middle term 'gentlemen' forming the subject is distributed. Since the middle term is distributed twice, the conclusion cannot be universal. Since one premise is negative, the conclusion must be negative. Thus, it follows that 'Some rich men are not poor'. Thus, neither I nor II follows.

question: 14. Statements: Some swords are sharp. All swords are rusty Conclusions:  eginenumerate*[! itemformat= rabic*. item Some rusty things are sharp. item Some rusty things are not sharp. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

question: Pointing to a photograph of a boy Suresh said, "He is the son of the only son of my mother." How is Suresh related to that boy? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The boy in the photograph is the only son of the son of Suresh's mother i.e., the son of Suresh. Hence, Suresh is the father of boy.

question: If A + B means A is the mother of B; A - B means A is the brother B; A % B means A is the father of B and A x B means A is the sister of B, which of the following shows that P is the maternal uncle of Q? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: P - M → P is the brother of M M + N → M is the mother of N N x Q → N is the sister of Q Therefore, P is the maternal uncle of Q.

question: If A is the brother of B; B is the sister of C; and C is the father of D, how D is related to A? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: If D is Male, the answer is Nephew. If D is Female, the answer is Niece. As the sex of D is not known, hence, the relation between D and A cannot be determined. Note: Niece - A daughter of one's brother or sister, or of one's brother-in-law or sister-in-law. Nephew - A son of one's brother or sister, or of one's brother-in-law or sister-in-law.

question: If A + B means A is the brother of B; A - B means A is the sister of B and A x B means A is the father of B. Which of the following means that C is the son of M? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: M x N → M is the father of N N - C → N is the sister of C and C + F → C is the brother of F. Hence, M is the father of C or C is the son of M.

question: 5. Introducing a boy, a girl said, "He is the son of the daughter of the father of my uncle." How is the boy related to the girl? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The father of the boy's uncle arrow the grandfather of the boy and daughter of the grandfather arrow sister of father.

question: Pointing to a photograph Lata says, "He is the son of the only son of my grandfather." How is the man in the photograph related to Lata? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The man in the photograph is the son of the only son of Lata's grandfather i.e., the man is the son of Lata's father. Hence, the man is the brother of Lata.

question: If A + B means A is the brother of B; A x B means A is the son of B; and A % B means B is the daughter of A then which of the following means M is the maternal uncle of N? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: Because the sex of O is not known.

question: 8. If D is the brother of B, how B is related to C? To answer this question which of the statements is/are necessary?  eginenumerate item The son of D is the grandson of C. item B is the sister of D. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: Given: D is the brother of B. From statement 1, we can detect that D is son of C (son of D is the grandson of C) . From statement 2, we can detect that B is 'Female' (sister of D) . Therefore, B is daughter of C.

question: If A + B means A is the father of B; A - B means A is the brother B; A % B means A is the wife of B and A x B means A is the mother of B, which of the following shows that M is the maternal grandmother of T? A.  B.  C.  D.
options: M x N x S % T
explanation: M x N → M is the mother of N N % S → N is the wife of S and S + T → is the father of T. Hence, M is the maternal grandmother of T.

question: Pointing to a photograph. Bajpai said, "He is the son of the only daughter of the father of my brother." How Bajpai is related to the man in the photograph? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The man in the photo is the son of the sister of Bajpai. Hence, Bajpai is the maternal uncle of the man in the photograph.

question: 11. Deepak said to Nitin, "That boy playing with the football is the younger of the two brothers of the daughter of my father's wife." How is the boy playing football related to Deepak? A.  B.  C.  D. -in-law
explanation: Father's wife → mother. Hence, the daughter of the mother means sister and sister's younger brother means brother. Therefore, the boy is the brother of Deepak.

question: 12. Pointing a photograph X said to his friend Y, "She is the only daughter of the father of my mother." How X is related to the person of photograph? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The only daughter of the father of X's mother means mother of X. Hence X is the son of the lady in the photograph. Note: Still have doubt like "How X is a male?" - Let's see the discussions.

question: 13. Veena who is the sister-in-law of Ashok, is the daughter-in-law of Kalyani. Dheeraj is the father of Sudeep who is the only brother of Ashok. How Kalyani is related to Ashok? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: Ashok is the only brother of Sudeep and Veena is the sister-in-law of Ashok. Hence Veena is the wife of Sudeep. Kalyani is the mother-in-law of Veena. Kalyani is the mother of Ashok.

question: If A + B means A is the sister of B; A x B means A is the wife of B, A % B means A is the father of B and A - B means A is the brother of B. Which of the following means T is the daughter of P? A.  B.  C.  D.
options: P x Q % R + S - T
P x Q % R - T + S
P x Q % R + T - S
P x Q % R + S + T
correct_answer: P x Q % R - T + S
explanation: P x Q → P is the wife of Q Q % R → Q is the father of R R - T → R is the brother of T T + S → T is the sister of S Therefore, T is the daughter of P.

question: Pointing to a woman, Abhijit said, "Her granddaughter is the only daughter of my brother." How is the woman related to Abhijit? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: Daughter of Abhijit's brother → niece of Abhijit. Thus the granddaughter of the woman is Abhijit's niece. Hence, the woman is the mother of Abhijit.

question: Amit said - "This girl is the wife of the grandson of my mother". How is Amit related to the girl? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The girl is the wife of grandson of Amit's mother i.e., the girl is the wife of son of Amit. Hence, Amit is the father-in-law of the girl.

question: 17.~ A and B are children of D. Who is the father of A? To answer this question which of the statements (1) and (2) is necessary? 1.~ C is the brother of A and the son of E. 2.~ F is the mother of B. A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: A and B are children of D. From (1) , C is the brother B and son of E. Since, the sex of D and E are not known. Hence (1) is not sufficient to answer the question. From (2) . F is the mother of B. Hence, F is also the mother of A. Hence D is the father of A. Thus, (2) is sufficient to answer the question.

question: Pointing towards a man, a woman said, "His mother is the only daughter of my mother." How is the woman related to the man? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: Only daughter of my mother → myself. Hence, the woman is the mother of the man.

question: If P Q means P is the brother of Q; P # Q means P is the mother of Q; P * Q means P is the daughter of Q in A # B C * D, who is the father? A. D B. B C. C D.
explanation: A is the mother of B, B is the brother of C and C is the daughter of D. Hence, D is the father.  eginarrayccc A & (Parents) & D | & & | | & & | B & - is - Brother - of - & C endarray

question: Introducing Sonia, Aamir says, "She is the wife of only nephew of only brother of my mother." How Sonia is related to Aamir? A.  B.  C. -in-law D.
explanation: Brother of mother means maternal uncle. Hence only nephew of Aamir's maternal uncle means Aamir himself. Therefore Sonia is the wife of Aamir.

question: If A + B means A is the brother of B; A % B means A is the father of B and A x B means A is the sister of B. Which of the following means M is the uncle of P? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: M + K → M is the brother of K K % T → K is the father of T T x P → T is the sister of P Therefore, K is the father of P and M is the uncle of P.

question: Pointing to Varman, Madhav said, "I am the only son of one of the sons of his father." How is Varman related to Madhav? A.  B.  C.  or  D.
explanation: Madhav is the only son of one of the sons of Varman's father → Either Varman is the father or uncle of Madhav.

question: 23. Introducing a woman, Shashank said, "She is the mother of the only daughter of my son." How that woman is related to Shashank? A.  B.  C.  D. -in-law
explanation: The woman is the mother of Shashank's granddaughter. Hence, the woman is the daughter-in-law of Shashank.

question: 24. If A + B means B is the brother of A; A x B means B is the husband of A; A - B means A is the mother of B and A % B means A is the father of B, which of the following relations shows that Q is the grandmother of T? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: Q - P → Q is the mother of P P + R → R is the brother of P Hence, → q is the mother of R R % T → R is the father of T. Hence, Q is the grandmother of T.

question: 25. A3P means A is the mother of P A4P means A is the brother of P A9P means A is the husband of P A5P means A is the daughter of P Which of the following means that K is the mother-in-law of M? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: M9N → M is the husband of N N5K → N is the daughter of K Hence, → M is the son-in-law of K K3J → K is the mother of J Hence, → K is a lady Hence, → K is the mother-in-law of M.

question: Pointing to a photograph Anjali said, "He is the son of the only son of my grandfather." How is the man in the photograph related to Anjali? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The man in the photograph is son of Anjali's grandfather's son i.e., the son of Anjali's father. Hence, the boy is the brother of Anjali.

question: 27. Pointing to a person, Deepak said, "His only brother is the father of my daughter's father". How is the person related to Deepak? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: Father of Deepak's daughter's father → Deepak's father. Hence, the person in the brother of Deepak's father. Therefore, the person is the uncle of Deepak.

question: P is the mother of K; K is the sister of D; D is the father of J. How is P related to J? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: P is the mother of K K is the sister of D D is the father of J Therefore, J is the nephew or niece of K and P is the grandmother of J

question: If P Q means P is the father of Q; P # Q means P is the mother of Q and P * Q means P is the sister of Q, then N # L P * Q shows which of the relation of Q to N? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: As the sex of Q is not known, hence, data is inadequate.

question: If A B means A is the brother of B; A @ B means A is the wife of B; A # B means A is the daughter of B and A * B means A is the father of B, which of the following indicates that U is the father-in-law of P? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: P @ Q → P is the wife of Q ... (1) Q T → Q is the brother of T ... (2) T # U → T is the daughter of U Hence, → Q is the son of U ... (3) U * W → U is the father of W. From (1) and (3) , U is the father-in-law of P.

question: 31. Introducing a man, a woman said, "He is the only son of the mother of my mother." How is the woman related to the man? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The man is the only son of the mother of the woman. Hence, the man is the maternal uncle of the woman. So, the woman is the niece of the man.

question: Pointing to Gopi, Nalni says, "I am the daughter of the only son of his grandfather." How Nalni is related to Gopi? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: Nalni is the daughter of the only son of Gopi's grandfather. Hence, it's clear that Nalni is the sister of Gopi.

question: 33. A's son B is married with C whose sister D is married to E the brother of B. How D is related to A? A.  B.  C. -in-law D.
explanation: Since E is the brother of B Therefore, A is the father of E but D is the wife of E. Hence, D is the daughter-in-law of A.

question: 34. Pointing to a lady a person said, "The son of her only brother is the brother of my wife." How is the lady related to the person? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: Brother of person's wife → brother-in-law of the person. Hence, the son of lady's brother is brother-in-law of the person. Therefore, the brother of the lady is the father-in-law of the person. Hence, the lady is the sister of the person's father-in-law.

question: 35. B5D means B is the father of D. B9D means B is the sister of D. B4D means B is the brother of D. B3D means B is the wife of D. Which of the following means F is the mother of K? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: F3M → F is the wife of M M5K → M is the father of K Therefore, F is the mother of K.

question: 36. A B means A is the father of B; A # B means A is the sister of B; A * B means A is the daughter of B and A @ B means A is the brother of B. Which of the following indicates that M is the wife of Q? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: Q R arrow Q is the father of R R @ T arrow R is the brother of T Hence, arrow Q is the father of T T * M arrow T is the daughter of M Hence, arrow M is the mother of T Hence, M is the wife of Q

question: If A B means A is the brother of B; B * C means B is the son of C; C @ D means C is the wife of D and A # D means A is the son of D, how C is related to A? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: A B → A is the brother of B B * C → B is the son of C Hence, → A is the son of C C @ D → C is the wife of D Hence, → C is the mother of A.

question: Pointing to a girl Sandeep said, "She is the daughter of the only sister of my father." How is sandeep related to the girl? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The girl is the daughter of the sister of Sandeep's father. Hence, the girl is the cousin or Sandeep is the cousin of the girl.

question: Pointing to a boy in the photograph Reena said, "He is the only son of the only child of my grandfather." How Reena is related to that boy? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The boy in the photograph is the only son of Reena's grandfather's only son; i.e., the boy is the only son of Reena's father. Hence, the boy is the brother of Reena or Reena is the sister of the boy.

question: If P % Q + R - T × K means P is the mother of Q, Q is the father of R, R is the brother of T, and T is the daughter of K, then the relation of P to K is A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: P % Q → P is the mother of Q Q + R → Q is the father of R R - T → R is the brother of T Hence, → Q is the father of T T x K → T is the daughter of K Hence, → Q is the husband of K. Therefore, P is the mother-in-law of K.

question: If P + Q means P is the brother of Q; P x Q means P is the father of Q and P - Q means P is the sister of Q, which of the following relations shows that I is the niece of K? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: K + Y → K is the brother of Y Y x I → Y is the father of I Hence, → K is the uncle of I and I - Z → I is the sister of Z Hence, → I is the niece of K.

question: 43. Pointing towards a girl, Abhisek says, "This girl is the daughter of only a child of my father." What is the relation of Abhisek's wife to that girl? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: Only the child of my father means 'Abhisek' himself. This means the girl is the daughter of Abhisek. Hence, Abhisek's wife is the mother of the girl.

question: 47. A is the son of C; C and Q are sisters; Z is the mother of Q and P is the son of Z. Which of the following statements is true? A.  B.  C.  D.
options: P is the maternal uncle of A
Q is the maternal grandfather of A
correct_answer: P is the maternal uncle of A
explanation: C and Q are sisters and A is the son of C. Hence, C is the mother of A or Z is the mother Q. Hence, Z is the maternal grandmother of A. P is the son of Z. Hence, P is the maternal uncle of A.

question: 48.~ M is the father of N who is the son of V. In order to know the relation of M to P, which of the statement/statements is/are necessary?  eginenumerate item P is the brother of V. item The daughter of N is the granddaughter of V. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: M is the father of N and N is the son of V. Hence, V is the mother of N. From (1) , P is the brother of V Therefore, M is the brother-in-law of P because V is the wife of M. From (2) , the daughter of N, is the granddaughter of V. From this we do not get any relation of M to P.

question: If X + Y means X is the daughter of Y; X - Y means X is the brother of Y; X % Y means X is the father of Y and X x Y means X is the sister of Y. Which of the following means I is the niece of J? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: I x C → I is the sister of C C + N → C is the daughter of N and N - J → N is the brother of I. Hence, I is niece of J.

question: 120, 99, 80, 63, 48, ? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The pattern is - 21, - 19, - 17, - 15,..... So, missing term = 48 - 13 = 35.

question: 4237, 897, 89, 9, ? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The digits are removed one by one from the beginning and the end in order alternately so as to obtain the subsequent terms of the series.

question: 3, 10, 101, ? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: Each term in the series is obtained by adding 1 to the square of the preceding term. So, missing term = (101) ^2 + 1 = 10202.

question: In the series 2, 6, 18, 54, ...... what will be the 8th term ? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: Clearly, 2 x 3 = 6, 6 x 3 = 18, 18 x 3 = 54,..... So, the series is a G.P. in which a = 2, r = 3. Therefore 8th term = ar^8-1 = ar^7 = 2 × 3^7 = (2 × 2187) = 4374.

question: 5. 5, 80, 45, 20, ? A. 5 B. 8 C.  D.
explanation: The pattern is - 45, - 35, - 25, ..... So, missing term = 20 - 15 = 5.

question: 6. 1, 1, 4, 8, 9, 27, 16, ? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The series consists of squares and cubes of consecutive natural numbers i.e. 1^2, 1^3, 2^2, 2^3, 3^2, 3^3, 4^2, ldots So, missing term = 4^3 = 64.

question: 7. 1, 2, 3, 6, 9, , ?, 54 A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The pattern is x 2, x /32, x 2, x /32, x 2, ldots So, missing term = 18 x /32 = 27

question: 8. 6, 13, 25, 51, 101, ? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The pattern is x 2 + 1, x 2 - 1, x 2 + 1, x 2 - 1, ldots So, missing term = 101 x 2 + 1 = 203

question: 10. 1, 3, 4, 8, 15, 27, ? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The sum of any three consecutive terms of the series gives the n term. So, missing number = 8 + 15 + 27 = 50.

question: 11. 3, 4, 7, 7, 13, 13, 21, 22, 31, 34, ? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The given sequence is a combination of two series : I. 3, 7, 13, 21, 31, ? and II. 4, 7, 13, 22, 34 The pattern in I is + 4, + 6, + 8, + 10,..... The pattern in II is + 3, + 6, + 9, + 12,..... So, missing term = 31 + 12 = 43.

question: . 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, ?, 17 A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: Clearly, the given series consists of prime numbers starting from 2. So, the missing term is the prime number after 11, which is 13.

question: 14. 6, 12, 21, ?, 48 A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The pattern is + 6, + 9, + 12, + 15, ... So, missing term = 21 + 12 = 33.

question: 15. Which term of the series 5, 10, 20, 40, ..... is 1280? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: Clearly, 5 x 2 = 10, 10 x 2 = 20, 20 x 2 = 40,..... So, the series is a G.P. in which a = 5 and r = 2. Let 1280 be the rath term of the series. Then, 5x2^n-1 = 1280 implies 2^n-1 = 256 = 2^8 implies n - 1 = 8 implies n = 9.

question: . 2, 5, 9, ?, 20, 27 A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The pattern is + 3, + 4, + 5, + 6,..... So, missing term = 9 + 5 = 14.

question: 17. 2, 3, 3, 5, 10, 13, ?, 43, 172, 177 A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The pattern is + 1, x 1, + 2, x 2, + 3, x 3, + 4, x 4, + 5. So, missing term = 13 x 3 = 39.

question: 18. 9, 27, 31, 155, 161, 1127, ? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: The pattern is x 3, + 4, x 5, + 6, x 7,..... So, missing term = 1127 + 8 = 1135.

question: 19. 2, 1, 2, 4, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 8, , , ? A. 9 B.  C.  D.
explanation: The given sequence is a combination of three series : I. 1st, 4th, 7th, 10th, 13th terms i.e. 2, 4, 6, 8, ? II. 2nd, 5th, 8th, 11th terms i.e. 1, 4, 7, 10 III. 3rd, 6th, 9th, 12th terms i.e. 2, 5, 8, 11 Clearly, I consists of consecutive even numbers. So, the missing term is 10.

question: 20. Which of the following will not be a number of the series 1, 8, 27, 64, 125,.....? A.  B.  C.  D.

question: 32. In the series 3, 9, 15, ...... what will be the 21st term? A.  B.  C.  D.
explanation: Clearly, 3 + 6 = 9, 9 + 6 = 15,..... So, the series is an A.P. in which a = 3 and d = 6. Therefore 21st term = a + (21 - 1) d = a + 20d = 3 + 20 x 6 = 123.

question: How many cubes having red, green and black colors on at least one side of the cube will be formed? A.  B.  C.  D. 4
explanation: Such cubes are related to the corners of the cuboid. Since the number of corners of the cuboid is 4. Hence, the number of such small cubes is 4.

question: How many small cubes will be formed? A. 6 B.  C.  D.
explanation: Number of small cubes = l × b × h = 6 × 4 × 1 = 24

question: How many cubes will have 4 coloured sides and two non-coloured sides? A. 8 B. 4 C.  D.

question: How many cubes will have green color on two sides and rest of the four sides having no color? A.  B.  C. 8 D. 4
explanation: There are 16 small cubes attached to the outer walls of the cuboid. Therefore remaining inner small cubes will be the cubes having two sides green coloured. So the required number = 24 - 16 = 8

question: How many cubes will remain if the cubes having black and green coloured are removed? A. 4 B. 8 C.  D.
explanation: Number of small cubes which are Black and Green is 8 in all. Hence, the number of remaining cubes are = 24 - 8 = 16

question: Question: In which year was Rahul born? Statements:  eginenumerate item Rahul at present is 25 years younger to his mother. item Rahul's brother, who was born in 1964, is 35 years younger to his mother. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: I alone is sufficient while II alone is not sufficient
II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
Either I or II is sufficient
Neither I nor II is sufficient
Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: From both I and II, we find that Rahul is (35 - 25) = 10 years older than his brother, who was born in 1964. So, Rahul was born in 1954.

question: 2. Question: What will be the total we of 10 poles, each of the same we? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. & One-fourth of the we of each pole is 5 kg. 2. & The total we of three poles is 20 kilograms more than the total we of two poles. endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: I alone is sufficient while II alone is not sufficient
II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
Either I or II is sufficient
Neither I nor II is sufficient
Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: Either I or II is sufficient
explanation: From I, we conclude that we of each pole = (4x5) kg = 20 kg. So, total we of 10 poles = (20 x 10) kg = 200 kg. From II, we conclude that: We of each pole = (we of 3 poles) - (we of 2 poles) = 20 kg. So, total we of 10 poles = (20 x 10) kg = 200 kg.

question: Question: How many children does M have? Statements:  eginenumerate item H is the only daughter of X who is wife of M. item K and J are brothers of M. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

question: Question: How much was the total sale of the company? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. The company sold 8000 units of product A each costing Rs. 25. &&& 2. This company has no other product line. &&& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
Either I or II is sufficient
Neither I nor II is sufficient
Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: From I, total sale of product A = Rs. (8000 x 25) = Rs. 200000. From II, we know that the company deals only in product A. This implies that sale of product A is the total sale of the company, which is Rs. 200000.

question: Question: The last Sunday of March, 2006 fell on which date? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. The first Sunday of that month fell on 5th. && 2. The last day of that month was Friday. && endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

Neither I nor II is sufficient

correct_answer: Either I or II is sufficient
explanation: From I, we conclude that 5th, 12th, 19th and 26th of March, 2006 were Sundays. So, the last Sunday fell on 26th. From II, we conclude that 31st March, 2006 was Friday. Thus, 26th March, 2006 was the last Sunday of the month.

question: Question: What is the code for 'sky' in the code language ? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. In the code language, 'sky is clear' is written as 'de ra fa'. &&& 2. In the same code language, 'make it clear' is written as 'de ga jo'. &&& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

question: Question: How many children are there between P and Q in a row of children? Statements:  eginenumerate item P is fifteenth from the in the row. item Q is exactly in the middle and there are ten children towards his r. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: From II, Q being in the middle, there are 10 children to his r as well as to his . So, Q is 11th from the . From I, P is 15th from the . Thus, from both I and II, we conclude that there are 3 children between P and Q.

question: Question: How is T related to K? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. & R's sister J has married T's brother L, who is the only son of his parents. 2. & K is the only daughter of L and J. endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: I alone is sufficient while II alone is not sufficient
II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
Either I or II is sufficient
Neither I nor II is sufficient
Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: From I, we know that L is T's brother and J's husband. Since L is the only son of his parents, T is L's sister. From II, we know that K is L's daughter. Thus, from I and II, we conclude that T is the sister of K's father i.e. T is K's aunt.

question: Question: How is J related to P? Statements:  eginenumerate*[!r] item M is brother of P and T is sister of P. item P's mother is married to J's husband who has one son and two daughters. endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

Neither I nor II is sufficient
Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
explanation: From II, we know that P's mother is married to J's husband, which means that J is P's mother.

question: Question: How is X related to Y? Statements:  eginenumerate item Y and Z are children of D who is wife of X. item R's sister X is married to Y's father. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

correct_answer: Either I or II is sufficient
explanation: From I, we conclude that Y is the child of D who is wife of X i.e. X is Y's father. From II, X is married to Y's father. This implies that X is Y's mother.

question: 11. Question: Who is to the immediate r of P among five persons P, Q, R, S and T facing North? Statements:  eginflalign* & 1. R is third to the of Q and P is second to the r of R.& & 2. Q is to the immediate of T who is second to the r of P.& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

question: Question: On which date of the month was Anjali born in February 2004? Statements:  eginflalign* & 1. Anjali was born on an even date of the month.& & 2. Anjali's birth date was a prime number.& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

question: Question: How is X related to Y? Statements:  eginenumerate*[!1] item Y says, "I have only one brother". item X says, "I have only one sister". endenumerate* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

question: Question: How is F related to P? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. P has two sisters M and N. &&& 2. F's mother is sister of M's father. &&& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

question: Question: B is the brother of A. How is A related to B ? Statements:  eginenumerate item A is the sister of C. item E is the husband of A. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

question: Question: How many children are there in the row of children facing North? Statements:  eginenumerate item Vishakha who is fifth from the end is eh to the of Ashish who is twelfth from the r end. item Rohit is fifth to the of Nisha who is seventh from the r end and eeenth from the end. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
Either I or II is sufficient
Neither I nor II is sufficient
Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: Either I or II is sufficient
explanation: Since 8th to the of 12th from the r is 20th from the r, so from I, we know that Vishakha is 5th from and 20th from r i.e. there are 4 children to the and 19 to the r of Vishakha. So, there are (4 + 1 + 19) i.e. 24 children in the row. From II, Nisha is 7th from r and 18th from end of the row. So, there are (6 + 1 + 17) = 24 children in the row.

question: 17. Question: How many doctors are p/tising in this town? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. There is one doctor per seven hundred residents. &&& 2. There are 16 wards with each ward having as many doctors as the number of wards. &&& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

explanation: From I, total number of doctors in town = ( /1700 × N ) , where N = total number of residents in town. But, the value of N is not known. From II, total number of doctors in town = (Number of wards in town) × (Number of doctors in each ward) = 16 × 16 = 256.

question: Question: On which day of the week was birthday of Sahil? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. Sahil celebrated his birthday the very n day on which Arun celebrated his birthday. &&& 2. The sister of Sahil was born on the third day of the week and two days after Sahil was born. &&& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: I alone is sufficient while II alone is not sufficient
II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
Either I or II is sufficient
Neither I nor II is sufficient
Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
explanation: I does not mention the day of the week on the birthday of either Arun or Sahil. According to II, Sahil's sister was born on Wednesday and Sahil was born two days before Wednesday i.e. on Monday.

question: Question: How many pages of book X did Robert read on Sunday? Statements: 1. The book has 300 pages out of which two-thirds were read by him before Sunday. 2. Robert read the last 40 pages of the book on the morning of Monday. A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: I alone is sufficient while II alone is not sufficient
II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
Either I or II is sufficient
Neither I nor II is sufficient
Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: From I and II, we find that Robert read (300 x 0.67) i.e. 200 pages before Sunday and the last 40 pages on Monday. This means that he read [300 - (200 + 40) ] i.e. 60 pages on Sunday.

question: Question: How is Tanya related to the man in the photograph? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. Man in the photograph is the only son of Tanya's grandfather. && 2. The man in the photograph has no brothers or sisters and his father is Tanya's grandfather. && endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
Either I or II is sufficient
Neither I nor II is sufficient
Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
explanation: From I, we conclude that the man is the only son of Tanya's grandfather i.e. he is Tanya's father or Tanya is the man's daughter. From II, we conclude that the man's father is Tanya's grandfather. Since the man has no brothers or sisters, so he is Tanya's father or Tanya is the man's daughter.

question: Question: Among T, V, B, E and C, who is the third from the top when arranged in the descending order of their wes? Statements:  eginflalign 1. B is heavier than T and C and is less heavier than V who is not the heaviest. && 2. C is heavier than only T. && endflalign A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: I alone is sufficient while II alone is not sufficient
II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
Either I or II is sufficient
Neither I nor II is sufficient
Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: I alone is sufficient while II alone is not sufficient
explanation: From I, we have: B > T, B > C, V > B. Thus, V is heavier than each one of B, T and C. But V is not the heaviest. So, E is the heaviest. Thus, we have the order. E > V > B > T > C or E > V > B > C > T. Clearly, B is third from the top.

question: Question: Which word in the code language means 'flower'? Statements: 1. 'de fu la pane' means 'rose flower is beautiful' and 'la quiz' means 'beautiful tree'. 2. 'de la chin' means 'red rose flower' and 'pa chin' means 'red tea'. A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: I alone is sufficient while II alone is not sufficient
II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
Either I or II is sufficient
Neither I nor II is sufficient
Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: Neither I nor II is sufficient
explanation: From the two statements given in I, the code for the only common word 'beautiful' can be determined. From the two statements given in II, the code for the only common word 'red' can be determined. In I and II, the common words are 'rose and 'flower' and the common code words are 'de' and 'la'. So, the code for 'flower' is either 'de' or 'la'.

question: Question: On a T.V. channel, four serials A, B, C and D were screened, one on each day, on four consecutive days but not necessarily in that order. On which day was the serial C screened? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. & The first serial was screened on 23rd, Tuesday and was followed by serial D.& 2. & Serial A was not screened on 25th and one serial was screened between serials A and B.& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: I alone is sufficient while II alone is not sufficient

Either I or II is sufficient

Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: From I, we know that the serials were screened on 23rd, 24th, 25th and 26th. Clearly, D was screened second i.e. on 24th, Wednesday. From II, we know that one serial was screened between A and B. So, A and B were screened first and third, i.e. on 23rd and 25th. But, A was not screened on 25th. So, A was screened on 23rd and B on 25th. Thus, C was screened on 26th, Friday.

question: Question: How is Sulekha related to Nandini? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. Sulekha's husband is the only son of Nandini's mother. &&& 2. Sulekha's brother and Nandini's husband are cousins. &&& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: I alone is sufficient while II alone is not sufficient
II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
Either I or II is sufficient
Neither I nor II is sufficient
Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: Either I or II is sufficient
explanation: From I, we conclude that Sulekha is the wife of Nandini's mother's only son i.e. Nandini's brother. Thus, Sulekha is Nandini's sister-in-law. From II, we conclude that Sulekha is the cousin of Nandini's husband, which implies that Sulekha is Nandini's sister-in-law.

question: 27. Question: Can Ritesh retire from office X in January 2006, with full pension benefits? Statements:  eginenumerate item Ritesh will complete 30 years of service in office X in April 2000 and desires to retire. item As per office X rules, an employee has to complete minimum 30 years of service and attain age of 60. Ritesh has 3 years to complete age of 60. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: I alone is sufficient while II alone is not sufficient
II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
Either I or II is sufficient
Neither I nor II is sufficient
Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: Clearly, the facts given in I and II contain two conditions to be fulfilled to get retirement and also indicate that Ritesh fulfills only one condition out of them.

question: Question: What is the code for 'or' in the code language? Statements: 1. 'nik sa te' means 'r or wrong', 'ro da nik' means 'he is r' and 'fe te ro' means 'that is wrong'. 2. 'pa nik la' means 'that r man', 'sa ne pa' means 'this or that' and 'ne ka re' means 'tell this there'. A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: I alone is sufficient while II alone is not sufficient
II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
Either I or II is sufficient
Neither I nor II is sufficient
Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: Either I or II is sufficient
explanation: I. In 'r or wrong' and 'he is r', the common word is 'r' and the common code word is 'nik'. So 'nik' means 'r'. In 'r or wrong' and 'that is wrong', the common word is 'wrong' and the common code word is 'te'. So, 'te' means 'wrong'. Thus, in 'r or wrong', 'sa' is the code for 'or'. II. In 'that r man' and 'this or that', the common word is 'that' and the common code word is 'pa'. So, 'pa' means 'that'. In 'this or that' and 'tell this there', the common word is 'this' and the common code word is 'ne'. So, 'ne' means 'this'. Thus, in 'this or that', 'sa' is the code for 'or'.

question: Question: Madan is elder than Kamal and Sharad is younger than Arvind. Who among them is the youngest ? Statements:  eginenumerate item Sharad is younger than Madan. item Arvind is younger than Kamal. endenumerate A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
options: I alone is sufficient while II alone is not sufficient
II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
Either I or II is sufficient
Neither I nor II is sufficient
Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
explanation: As given, we have: M > K, A > S. From II, K > A. Thus, we have: M > K > A > S. So, Sharad is the youngest. From I, M > S. Thus, we have: M > K > A > S or M > A > K > S or M > A > S > K.

question: Question: On which date in August was Kapil born? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. Kapil's mother remembers that Kapil was born before nineteenth but after fifteenth. && 2. Kapil's brother remembers that Kapil was born before seventeenth but after twelfth. && endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

II alone is sufficient while I alone is not sufficient
Either I or II is sufficient
Neither I nor II is sufficient
Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: From I, we conclude that Kapil was born on any one of the dates among 16th, 17th and 18th. From II, we conclude that Kapil was born on any one of the dates among 13th, 14th, 15th and 16th. Thus, from both I and II, we conclude that Kapil was born on 16th August.

question: Question: What is Gagan's age? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. & Gagan, Vimal and Kunal are all of the same age.& 2. & Total age of Vimal, Kunal and Anil is 32 years and Anil is as old as Vimal and Kunal together.& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

Neither I nor II is sufficient
Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: 答案是:E 解析如下: As given in I and II, we have: G = V = K, V + K + A = 32 and A = V + K. Putting V + K = A in V + K + A = 32, we have: 2A = 32 or A = 16. Thus, V + K = 16 and V = K. So, V = K = 8. Thus, G = 8.

question: Question: In a certain code, '13' means 'stop smoking' and '59' means 'injurious habit'. What do '9' and '5' mean respectively in that code ? Statements:  eginflalign* & 1. '157' means 'stop bad habit'.& & 2. '839' means 'smoking is injurious'.& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

Neither I nor II is sufficient

correct_answer: Either I or II is sufficient
explanation: 59 means injurious habit and 157 means stop bad habit (from I) . Thus, the common code number 5 stands for common word habit. So, 9 represents injurious. Hence, I is sufficient. Also, 59 means injurious habit and 839 means smoking is injurious. Thus, the common code number 9 stands for common word injurious. So, 5 represents habit. Thus, II is also sufficient.

question: 33. Question: How much money do Vivek and Suman have together? Statements:  eginflalign* & 1. Suman has 20 rupees less than what Tarun has.& & 2. Vivek has 30 rupees more than what Tarun has.& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

question: Question: Among Monika, Anita, Sonal, Ratna and Tanvy, who came last for the programme? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. & Monika came after Anita but not after Tanvy. 2. & Ratna came after Tanvy but not after Sonal. endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

question: Question: Who among P, Q, R, S and T is the lest? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. R & is heavier than Q and T but ler than S.& 2. S & is not the heaviest.& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

Both I and II are sufficient
correct_answer: Neither I nor II is sufficient
explanation: From I, we have: R > Q, R > T, S > R i.e. S > R > Q > T or S > R > T > Q. From II, S is not the heaviest. So, P is the heaviest. Thus, we have: P > S > R > Q > T or P > S > R > T > Q. Hence, either T or Q is the lest.

question: Question: How is T related to K? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. K has two sons; one of the sons is A. && 2. The mother of T has only two sons - A and B. && endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

question: Question: What is the shortest distance between Devipur and Durgapur? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. Durgapur is 20 kms away from Rampur. && 2. Devipur is 15 kms away from Rampur. && endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

question: 39. Question: Manoj, Prabhakar, Akash and Kamal are four friends. Who among them is the heaviest? Statements:  eginflalign* & 1. Prabhakar is heavier than Manoj and Kamal but ler than Akash.& & 2. Manoj is ler than Prabhakar and Akash but heavier than Kamal.& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

question: Question: Vinod's and Javed's salaries are in the proportion of 4 : 3 respectively. What is Vinod's salary? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. & Javed's salary is 75% that of Vinod's salary.& 2. & Javed's salary is Rs 4500.& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.

explanation: Statement I is merely an interpretation of the information contained in the question. However, Vinod's salary can be ascertained from II as follows : Let Vinod's and Javed's salaries be 4x and 3x respectively. Then, 3x = 4500 or x = 1500. Therefore Vinod's salary = 4x = Rs. 6000.

question: Question: What is Nitin's rank from the top in a class of forty students? Statements:  eginflalign* 1. There are ten students between Nitin and Deepak. &&& 2. Deepak is twentieth from the top. &&& endflalign* A.  B.  C.  D.  E.
